section.title: Introduction
section.para: Real-world text carries damage: characters a broken decode left behind, control bytes from binary spillage, invisible characters hiding or reordering content, and words mangled by reading UTF-8 bytes through a legacy single-byte decoding. The package opennlp.tools.artifacts classifies such stretches as spans over the original, unmodified text. Detection never rewrites anything: a finding states where the problem is and what kind it is, and the caller decides whether to mask, drop, or repair, with the original offsets intact.

The built-in detector reports these types, each a constant on TextArtifact: replacement (U+FFFD runs), control (control characters that are not whitespace), noncharacter, unpaired-surrogate, private-use, bidi-control (explicit directional overrides and isolates), zero-width (zero-width characters outside their orthographic uses; joiners in emoji sequences and single occurrences between letters are kept), and mojibake (a stretch whose single-byte image is valid multi-byte UTF-8, the signature of a double decode).

section.title: Detecting artifacts
The artifacts layer
section.para:
ArtifactAnnotator adapts the detector to the document pipeline. It requires no other layer and provides opennlp:artifacts, one annotation per finding carrying its TextArtifact:
Document document = new ArtifactAnnotator() .annotate(Document.of("cafÃ© costs �8"));
List<Annotation<TextArtifact>> artifacts = document.get(ArtifactAnnotator.ARTIFACTS); // two annotations; the first covers "Ã©" and carries type "mojibake"
Because findings are exact spans on a layer, any layer consumer composes with them: masking replaces the covered characters, filtering drops annotations by type, and a cleanup step can rewrite the flagged stretches while recording its edits.
